# GROUNDWORK.md
# Generated by Groundwork

## Schema: storefront_system
E-commerce platform where users can create products for their storefronts and manage shopping carts

> 5 tables · 29 columns · 5 relationships

---

## Tables

### users
Platform users who can create storefronts and products

| Column | Type | Constraints |
|--------|------|-------------|
| id | integer | primary key |
| email | string | unique |
| name | string |  |
| created_at | timestamp | default: now() |

### storefronts
User-owned storefronts that display products

| Column | Type | Constraints |
|--------|------|-------------|
| id | integer | primary key |
| user_id | integer | fk → users.id |
| name | string |  |
| description | text |  |
| created_at | timestamp | default: now() |

### products
Products created by users and displayed in storefronts

| Column | Type | Constraints |
|--------|------|-------------|
| id | integer | primary key |
| storefront_id | integer | fk → storefronts.id |
| name | string |  |
| description | text |  |
| size | string |  |
| weight | float |  |
| quantity | integer |  |
| price | float |  |
| created_at | timestamp | default: now() |

### shopping_carts
User shopping carts containing products

| Column | Type | Constraints |
|--------|------|-------------|
| id | integer | primary key |
| user_id | integer | fk → users.id |
| created_at | timestamp | default: now() |

### cart_items
Products added to shopping carts with specific attributes

| Column | Type | Constraints |
|--------|------|-------------|
| id | integer | primary key |
| cart_id | integer | fk → shopping_carts.id |
| product_id | integer | fk → products.id |
| size | string |  |
| weight | float |  |
| quantity | integer |  |
| price | float |  |
| created_at | timestamp | default: now() |

---

## Relationships
- **users → storefronts**: one-to-many via `storefronts.user_id` — Users can own multiple storefronts
- **storefronts → products**: one-to-many via `products.storefront_id` — Storefronts can contain multiple products
- **shopping_carts → users**: one-to-one via `shopping_carts.user_id` — Each user has one shopping cart
- **shopping_carts → cart_items**: one-to-many via `cart_items.cart_id` — Shopping carts can contain multiple items
- **cart_items → products**: many-to-many via `cart_items.product_id` — Products can be added to multiple carts and carts can contain multiple products

## Relationships Map

```
  users 1──* storefronts (user_id)
  storefronts 1──* products (storefront_id)
  shopping_carts 1──1 users (user_id)
  shopping_carts 1──* cart_items (cart_id)
  cart_items *──* products (product_id)
```

---

## Example Queries

**users**

```sql
-- Get all users
SELECT * FROM users;

-- Get users by ID
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?;

-- Create a new record
INSERT INTO users (email, name, created_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?);
```

**storefronts**

```sql
-- Get all storefronts
SELECT * FROM storefronts;

-- Get storefronts by ID
SELECT * FROM storefronts WHERE id = ?;

-- Create a new record
INSERT INTO storefronts (user_id, name, description, created_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?);

-- Get storefronts with users info
SELECT t.*, r.*
FROM storefronts t
JOIN users r ON t.user_id = r.id
WHERE t.id = ?;
```

**products**

```sql
-- Get all products
SELECT * FROM products;

-- Get products by ID
SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = ?;

-- Create a new record
INSERT INTO products (storefront_id, name, description, size, weight, quantity, price, created_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?);

-- Get products with storefronts info
SELECT t.*, r.*
FROM products t
JOIN storefronts r ON t.storefront_id = r.id
WHERE t.id = ?;
```

**shopping_carts**

```sql
-- Get all shopping_carts
SELECT * FROM shopping_carts;

-- Get shopping_carts by ID
SELECT * FROM shopping_carts WHERE id = ?;

-- Create a new record
INSERT INTO shopping_carts (user_id, created_at) VALUES (?, ?);

-- Get shopping_carts with users info
SELECT t.*, r.*
FROM shopping_carts t
JOIN users r ON t.user_id = r.id
WHERE t.id = ?;
```

**cart_items**

```sql
-- Get all cart_items
SELECT * FROM cart_items;

-- Get cart_items by ID
SELECT * FROM cart_items WHERE id = ?;

-- Create a new record
INSERT INTO cart_items (cart_id, product_id, size, weight, quantity, price, created_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?);

-- Get cart_items with shopping_carts info
SELECT t.*, r.*
FROM cart_items t
JOIN shopping_carts r ON t.cart_id = r.id
WHERE t.id = ?;

-- Get cart_items with products info
SELECT t.*, r.*
FROM cart_items t
JOIN products r ON t.product_id = r.id
WHERE t.id = ?;
```

---

## Common Mistakes

- When querying `storefronts` with `user_id`, always JOIN on `users.id` — do not select `user_id` as a raw integer
- When querying `products` with `storefront_id`, always JOIN on `storefronts.id` — do not select `storefront_id` as a raw integer
- When querying `shopping_carts` with `user_id`, always JOIN on `users.id` — do not select `user_id` as a raw integer
- When querying `cart_items` with `cart_id`, always JOIN on `shopping_carts.id` — do not select `cart_id` as a raw integer
- When querying `cart_items` with `product_id`, always JOIN on `products.id` — do not select `product_id` as a raw integer
- The column is `storefronts.user_id`, not `storefronts.user` — always use the exact column name
- The column is `products.storefront_id`, not `products.storefront` — always use the exact column name
- The column is `shopping_carts.user_id`, not `shopping_carts.user` — always use the exact column name
- The column is `cart_items.cart_id`, not `cart_items.shopping_cart` — always use the exact column name
- The column is `cart_items.product_id`, not `cart_items.product` — always use the exact column name

## Instructions

This file is the source of truth for the database schema. You MUST follow these rules:

1. Use the exact table and column names defined in this file — do not guess or infer names
2. Use the relationships defined above — do not invent new foreign keys or associations
3. When JOINing tables, use the foreign keys listed in the Relationships section
4. Include all non-nullable columns when inserting records; let defaults handle timestamps
5. Use indexed columns (primary keys, foreign keys) in WHERE clauses for performance
6. Check the Common Mistakes section before writing any query involving these tables
7. If a column is marked nullable, handle NULL values explicitly (COALESCE, IS NULL checks, or conditional logic)
